metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `All-Projects` provisioning fork: name the single file (`infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh` is the better choice since `setup-project.sh` is documented as managing only the `rebar` project) and add an AC proving command (e.g. `git ls-remote gerrit All-Projects refs/meta/config | ssh gerrit gerrit ls-projects` or a `git show refs/meta/config:project.config | grep rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader`) that confirms the grant is live.
findings.finding: The provisioning path for the `All-Projects` `[capability]` grant is a fork ('extend `setup-project.sh` or add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`'), deferring a real file/location decision to the executor.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh` (not an extension of the rebar-scoped script), include the exact `[capability]` stanza in the plan, and add an AC proving command: `ssh -p 29418 gerrit gerrit ls-members --recursive 'Service Users' | grep autolander-bot && git archive --remote=ssh://gerrit:29418/All-Projects refs/meta/config project.config | tar -xO | grep -A2 rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/autolander/loop.py` (and any companion HTTP server module) to the Files section as an edited file, annotated with which ACs (AC5 heartbeat write, AC6 status endpoint + time-to-land metric, AC7 sentinel check + drain) it covers.
findings.finding: The Scope section explicitly states that AC5 (heartbeat write), AC6 (queue-status endpoint + time-to-land metric emission), and AC7 (emergency-stop sentinel check, drain-on-shutdown) 'live in the same service module (`infra/autolander/loop.py`)' — but `infra/autolander/loop.py` does not appear anywhere in the Files section (neither as a new file nor as an edited file). This is a direct cross-section contradiction between Scope and Files, and an E1 orphan: three ACs describe deliverables in a specific named file that the implementation file list omits entirely.
findings.location: Scope section ('live in the same service module (`infra/autolander/loop.py`)') vs Files section (no entry for loop.py)
findings.suggested_fix: Under Files > Existing files edited, add: `infra/autolander/loop.py` — add heartbeat timestamp write on each poll iteration (AC5), stat the emergency-stop sentinel each iteration and transition to `paused` phase (AC7), emit time-to-land `PutMetricData` on successful submit (AC6); and if the HTTP status server is a separate module, add `infra/autolander/status_server.py` (new) serving the AC6 JSON endpoint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the forked edit target for the All-Projects [capability] provisioning: designate exactly one file (either extend infra/gerrit/setup-project.sh with an All-Projects clone+push step, or create infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh) as the canonical script, and add it explicitly to the Files edit set.
findings.finding: The All-Projects provisioning step has an unresolved forked edit target: the plan says 'extend setup-project.sh or add infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh' without committing to either, so no single file is definitively in the edit set. Neither infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh (does not exist) nor a concrete decision to extend setup-project.sh is made. This means the implementer has no designated file to edit or create.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the non-terminal drain path in AC7: define the recovery file's path (e.g. a `wipchain-recovery.json` in the state volume), format (the full wipChain schema fields), and the restarted instance's startup behaviour (check for recovery file → resume from saved phase rather than selecting from scratch) — distinct from the S3 outcome marker which is only written on terminal resolution.
findings.finding: The drain-on-SIGTERM description in AC7 conflates two distinct drain outcomes under the same 'write the S3 outcome marker' language. For a wipChain that COMPLETES within the drain window, writing the S3 outcome marker is correct. But for a wipChain still blocked on CI when the deadline expires, the plan says 'record its state and exit anyway' without specifying what format or file that state record takes. If the restarted instance interprets the absence of a recovery record as 'no in-flight work', it will drop the partially-processed stack and re-select from the queue — potentially re-driving a stack that already had its Autosubmit label removed or its tested SHAs reset. The plan leaves this recovery contract underspecified, creating a hidden ordering assumption that the restart instance reads the wipChain recovery file correctly.
findings.location: AC7 (Emergency-stop + drain) — 'if the in-flight wipChain is still blocked on CI past the deadline, record its state and exit anyway rather than blocking the redeploy indefinitely'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the exact `--metric-name` string for the per-landed-change time-to-land duration metric to AC6 (e.g. `autolander_land_duration_s`) so `loop.py` and `monitoring_autolander.tf` share the same name without coordinator.
findings.finding: AC6 specifies the bot emits a 'raw per-landed-change queue-wait/resident-time duration' via `PutMetricData` as individual `Value` datapoints, but the metric name (the exact `--metric-name` string) is never stated anywhere in the plan. Every other metric defined in this plan and codebase has an explicit name (`autolander_errors`, `voter_errors`, `replication_errors`, etc.). Without the name, the `monitoring_autolander.tf` alarm reference and any `observability.sh` feeder cannot be consistently wired across files.
findings.location: AC6 (Queue-status + liveness surface) — Time-to-land metric paragraph

findings.finding: The Gerrit provisioning path for the All-Projects capability grant is an unresolved OR: 'extend setup-project.sh or add infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh'. An executor must make a design choice that is architectural (one file vs. a new file with its own invocation wiring).

The Files section leaves the Gerrit provisioning file undetermined ('extend `setup-project.sh` or add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`'), while AC2 treats the All-Projects capability grant as a concrete deliverable that must be verified. This creates a cross-section gap: AC2 specifies what must be provisioned but the Files section does not commit to which file delivers it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for the Gerrit capability grant: e.g. after provisioning, run `ssh -p 29418 gerrit gerrit ls-members 'Service Users' | grep autolander-bot` AND make one test `POST /a/changes/{id}/rebase` call with the autolander-bot credential against a scratch change to confirm the 200/409 (not 403) response.
findings.finding: No proving command verifies that the `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` capability and submit permission are actually effective for `autolander-bot` — the verification section only checks 'authenticates (SSM token → .env → HTTP)', which passes even if the Gerrit account lacks the required capability.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verification step in AC8 that, during the live container boot, curls the status endpoint and asserts (a) HTTP 200, (b) JSON body contains `heartbeat_age_s` as an integer key, and (c) the field is present even before the first landed change (empty wipChain case).
findings.finding: The HTTP status endpoint (AC6) — a new user-facing API whose `heartbeat_age_s` field S4's `lander_down` directly binds to — has no test coverage in AC8 whatsoever: no happy-path assertion, no schema validation, and no test of the empty/startup state (e.g., no wipChain yet). If the field name is wrong or the JSON shape diverges from the contract, the test suite goes green while S4 silently misreads liveness.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC8 (Verification/tests), cross-referenced with AC6 (Queue-status endpoint)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC8, add: 'The live boot test curls the status endpoint (`curl -s http://localhost:<port>/status`) and asserts: HTTP 200, JSON body contains `heartbeat_age_s` (integer), `wip_chain` (null or object), and `autosubmit_waiting` (integer). Record the curl output in the DD entry as observable proof.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verification step in AC8 that sends SIGTERM while the bot has a simulated in-flight wipChain blocked past the drain deadline, and asserts the service exits within the deadline window AND has written the outcome marker before exiting.
findings.finding: AC7 defines a time-bounded drain: if the in-flight wipChain is still blocked on CI past the SIGTERM deadline, the service must record state and exit anyway. AC8 only tests the happy-path drain ('drain-on-shutdown records the in-flight outcome') and never tests the timeout/forced-exit branch, leaving the deadline logic unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC8 (Verification/tests), cross-referenced with AC7 (Emergency-stop + drain)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC8, add: 'Drain-timeout test: inject a mock wipChain that never completes; send SIGTERM; assert the process exits within (deadline + 5 s); assert the S3/state outcome marker is written before exit. Record exit time and marker presence in the DD entry.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the fork in the Files section: pick ONE of `extend setup-project.sh` or `add infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`, add it as a concrete named file with the exact `[capability]` stanza (`rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader = group Service Users` or the named bot group), and remove the 'or' deferral so the AC2 server-level grant has a reproducible, automated provisioning path.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `setup-project.sh` manages 'only the `rebar` project's `refs/meta/config`' (verified true), then concludes the `All-Projects` `[capability]` grant for `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` needs 'a named provisioning step (extend `setup-project.sh` or add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`)'. However, neither option is decided — it is left as 'exact... provisioning path is the operator-cutover surface'. No concrete file is added to the Files list for this step. The scope exclusion 'the exact stanza and provisioning path is the operator-cutover surface' is used to DELETE the work from the plan's deliverables, but a false exclusion here means the `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` grant has no automated provisioning path at all, leaving the bot unable to rebase on behalf of uploaders on the live server.
findings.location: Files section / infra/gerrit/ provisioning bullet (All-Projects [capability] grant)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh` to the Files section as a new file that uses `git fetch/checkout refs/meta/config` on `All-Projects`, appends `[capability]\n\trebaseOnBehalfOfUploader = group Service Users` if absent, and pushes — following the same clone→edit→push pattern as `setup-project.sh`. Register it in the operator cutover runbook.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is a server-level capability granted in `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config` under `[capability]`, but no provisioning script or config snippet for this grant is specified, and there is zero prior use of `[capability]` blocks in the repo's `infra/gerrit/` scripts. The `setup-project.sh` only manages the `rebar` project's `refs/meta/config`, not `All-Projects`. This is a new integration surface — Unverified at the config-syntax and provisioning-path level — and a capability gap: the plan names the requirement but does not name which script adds it or provide the exact `[capability]` / `[access]` stanza.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to a specific file path for the All-Projects provisioning step (either extend `infra/gerrit/setup-project.sh` with an `All-Projects` section, or create `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`), include it in the plan's new-files or edited-files list, and document the runbook step that invokes it so the `[capability]` grant is reproducible-as-code rather than a one-time manual push.
findings.finding: The All-Projects `[capability]` / `[access]` provisioning step is explicitly left unresolved between two options ('extend `setup-project.sh` or add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`'). Because no committed script name or file path is specified, this provisioning cannot be CI-enforced via `config-check.sh` (check 4 validates shell-script syntax for every `infra/**/*.sh` — but a script that doesn't exist yet won't be checked), and the change is not reproducible-as-code. An admin-only out-of-band push to `All-Projects` without a codified script is the exact drift class this stack has runbooks to avoid.
findings.location: Files section — 'infra/gerrit/ provisioning — the All-Projects refs/meta/config [capability] grant … needs a named provisioning step (extend setup-project.sh or add infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh) — the exact [capability]/[access] stanza and provisioning path is the operator-cutover surface'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC6: specify the endpoint port and path (e.g. `http://localhost:9090/status`), then add a `set_verify_commands` entry: `curl -sf http://localhost:9090/status | jq -e '.heartbeat_age_s | type == "number" and . < 90'` and `jq -e '.wipChain and .queue_depth'` to assert required fields are present.
findings.finding: The AC6 read-only HTTP status endpoint has no concrete proving command — no `curl` invocation or response-shape assertion is specified to confirm it is reachable and returns the required JSON fields (`wipChain`, count, `heartbeat_age_s`).
findings.location: AC6 — 'a read-only HTTP status endpoint on the bot … serving JSON'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to plan: 'Endpoint: `GET http://localhost:9090/status` (port 9090 configurable via env `AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT`). Proving command: `curl -sf http://localhost:9090/status | jq -e \'[.heartbeat_age_s, .queue_depth, .wipChain] | all(. != null)\'`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for the autoheal restart path: after making the heartbeat stale (e.g. `touch -t 197001010000 <statevol>/heartbeat`), wait for HEALTHCHECK to mark the container unhealthy, then assert `docker inspect autolander --format '{{.RestartCount}}'` increments by 1 within the autoheal poll interval.
findings.finding: No proving command verifies that the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar actually restarts the autolander container when it transitions to `unhealthy` — the verification only confirms HEALTHCHECK marks the container unhealthy, not that autoheal triggers a restart.
findings.location: AC4 — 'the HEALTHCHECK marks unhealthy on a hang … the willfarrell/autoheal sidecar … restarts any container labeled autoheal=true when it goes unhealthy'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `set_verify_commands`: `touch -t 197001010000 /var/gerrit/site/autolander/heartbeat && sleep 60 && docker inspect autolander --format '{{.RestartCount}}' | grep -E '^[1-9]'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for the AC3 unhealthy feeder: force the container to `unhealthy` (stale heartbeat), then assert `aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics --namespace rebar/host --metric-name autolander_errors … | jq -e '.Datapoints | length > 0'` confirms a datapoint was published within the polling window.
findings.finding: The AC3 `docker inspect` unhealthy feeder in `observability.sh` has no proving command confirming it emits a `PutMetricData` call when the container actually goes unhealthy — the plan describes the probe shape but provides no end-to-end test of the metric publication path.
findings.location: AC3 — 'The unhealthy feeder must therefore poll `docker inspect --format …` … a different probe shape — the probe is … in `observability.sh`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-response contract for the AC6 status endpoint: define the HTTP status code and JSON body (or explicit absence) returned during container startup (before first heartbeat write), during SIGTERM drain, and on internal error — so S4's lander_down has a deterministic fallback for each case.
findings.finding: The read-only HTTP status endpoint (AC6) specifies its happy-path JSON schema (wipChain, count, heartbeat_age_s) but does not define the error-response contract — what HTTP status code and body the endpoint returns when the service is starting up, shutting down (drain in progress), or otherwise degraded. S4's lander_down binds directly to the `heartbeat_age_s` field; if the endpoint returns a 5xx, an empty body, or a partial JSON object during startup/drain, S4's parsing and 'down' decision are undefined from S5's perspective.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC6: 'The endpoint returns HTTP 200 with the JSON schema at all times the process is running; during startup before the first heartbeat write, `heartbeat_age_s` is omitted or set to a sentinel (e.g. `null`) that S4 treats as `lander_down`; during drain the endpoint continues to serve the last known state; on unrecoverable internal error the endpoint returns HTTP 503 with `{"status": "error"}`. S4 must treat any non-200 response or missing `heartbeat_age_s` field as equivalent to `lander_down`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the AC3 spec, explicitly state the polling cadence for the `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'` unhealthy feeder relative to the observability timer period, and confirm the host-level observability script has Docker socket access (or specify a `docker events` subscription alternative with its teardown/timeout semantics).
findings.finding: The plan asserts the `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` journald marker 'matches the existing `VOTER_ERROR`/`G2P` pattern' for the observability.sh feeder, but the observability.sh file currently has NO section for `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` and no offset file for it — this is expected for a new story, but the plan's description of the probe shape uses the phrase 'matching the existing... pattern' as if the wiring already exists. More materially, the plan says the `unhealthy` Docker health-state feeder 'must poll `docker inspect --format ...`' — but no such `docker inspect` poll exists anywhere in `observability.sh` today, and the plan provides no specification of how frequently this poll runs relative to the existing timer cadence, nor how the Docker socket is accessible from the host-level observability script.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the AC6 status endpoint's exact binding (port + whether loopback-only or VPC-reachable), whether it is proxied by nginx (and if so, add the nginx stanza to the edited-files list), and the human/admin access-control: either a named auth mechanism (e.g., same Gerrit OAuth session, HTTP Basic, IP allowlist) or an explicit justified no-auth rationale (e.g., 'bound to 127.0.0.1 only, not proxied externally, therefore loopback-only with no public exposure').
findings.finding: The AC6 read-only HTTP status endpoint is a new network-reachable service stood up by this plan, but the plan does not commit to a specific binding (port/path), does not list nginx as an edited file, and does not state a named human/admin authentication mechanism or provide a justified no-auth rationale tied to a concrete access-control (e.g., 'loopback-only, not proxied by nginx, no public exposure'). The hedged phrase 'localhost/VPC or behind the same access as the Gerrit web' leaves the access boundary ambiguous — VPC-reachable without auth is a different posture from loopback-only.
findings.location: AC6 — 'a read-only HTTP status endpoint on the bot … bound so agents can reach it (localhost/VPC or behind the same access as the Gerrit web)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to AC6 and the Files section: bind the endpoint to `127.0.0.1:<port>` (loopback-only), add a `location /autolander/` proxy block to `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` behind Gerrit's existing GitHub OAuth session (so only authenticated Gerrit users can reach it), and add `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` to the edited-files list. This closes the access-contract gap and satisfies T10(b2) with a named auth mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC6: Declare the status endpoint's access posture explicitly — state whether it binds to `127.0.0.1` only (VPC/localhost-internal, no nginx proxy) or is internet-reachable (in which case require an authentication contract or rationale for public access); do not leave 'localhost/VPC or behind the same access as the Gerrit web' as the spec.
findings.finding: The AC6 read-only HTTP status endpoint has an undeclared access posture: the plan says 'localhost/VPC or behind the same access as the Gerrit web' — but Gerrit web IS proxied at the root of the public domain (rebar.solutions.navateam.com) via nginx. This leaves it ambiguous whether the status endpoint will be internet-reachable (via an nginx proxy_pass stanza) or VPC-internal-only (bound to 127.0.0.1, no nginx route). An undeclared posture on a network-reachable endpoint that exposes wipChain state, tested_shas, and heartbeat_age_s is the finding.
findings.location: AC6 — 'Queue-status + liveness surface': 'bound so agents can reach it (localhost/VPC or behind the same access as the Gerrit web)'
findings.suggested_fix: Bind the status endpoint to `127.0.0.1:<port>` (loopback only, not `0.0.0.0`) and declare in AC6: 'internal-only — loopback-bound, no nginx proxy route; accessible only from the host or via SSM Session Manager port-forward.' This removes the ambiguity and keeps wipChain state off the public internet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the aggregation protocol for the dual-feeder `autolander_errors` metric in AC3: state whether the two probe shapes emit a single combined `PutMetricData` call (with a defined summation formula and separate offset files) or two calls sharing the same metric name, and add a note matching the existing `observability.sh` offset-delta pattern for each feeder.
findings.finding: AC3/AC4 specify a single `rebar/host:autolander_errors` metric fed from TWO different probe shapes — (1) an offset-delta count of `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` journald lines and (2) a `docker inspect` / `docker events` poll for container `unhealthy` state transitions — but the aggregation protocol is never defined: are they two separate `PutMetricData` calls that CloudWatch sums? One combined delta? Are separate offset files used per feeder? The existing pattern in `observability.sh` uses one offset file and one `PutMetricData` call per metric source; combining two dissimilar probe shapes into one metric name without a defined protocol leaves the executor to invent the combination rule.
findings.suggested_fix: Use two separate offset files and sum the two deltas into a single `PutMetricData --value $((err_new + unhealthy_new))` call per interval, mirroring the shape of existing single-source feeders but with explicit per-source offset tracking. Document this in a comment block in `observability.sh`.

findings.finding: The AC6 read-only HTTP status endpoint's access posture is undeclared: the plan says 'localhost/VPC or behind the same access as the Gerrit web' without specifying the actual binding (loopback vs Docker-network-published), whether nginx will proxy it (and at what path), or whether any authentication is required — leaving it ambiguous whether the endpoint is internet-reachable and unauthenticated.
findings.location: AC6 — 'Queue-status + liveness surface' / Files section ('exposing the read-only status endpoint per AC6')
findings.suggested_fix: Declare one of: (a) loopback-only binding (`127.0.0.1:PORT:PORT` in docker-compose.yml) with an nginx proxy location `/autolander/status` → `http://127.0.0.1:PORT/` behind TLS (same pattern as `/review/`), access class = 'internal-VPC, no authn required, non-secret queue state' — document rationale; or (b) no port published outside the Docker network, accessible only from sibling containers, with S4's lander_down reading it via the internal Docker network name. Whichever is chosen, add an nginx location block (or explicitly omit one) and annotate the compose port mapping.

findings.finding: The `autolander_errors` metric combines two feeders with incompatible probe shapes (journald grep vs. `docker inspect` poll), but the plan never specifies the aggregation/counting rule for the `docker inspect` feeder: at what interval it polls, whether it emits a delta count (like the other feeders) or a per-transition count, and whether a hang that simultaneously triggers AUTOLANDER_ERROR in journald AND an `unhealthy` Docker state double-counts into the single metric. This is a decision rule that must be co-located with the metric schema but is absent.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'The unhealthy feeder is edge-triggered: it emits 1 count per `healthy→unhealthy` transition (using `docker events --filter event=health_status --filter event=die` piped to a background listener), stored in its own offset file at `/var/lib/rebar/autolander-unhealthy-offset`. This avoids double-counting with the journald feeder (a hang may write AUTOLANDER_ERROR AND go unhealthy; the journald feeder counts the log line, the unhealthy feeder counts the state transition — both legitimately increment the single alarm metric).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the SIGTERM drain deadline as a concrete value (e.g. 'drain deadline = 25 minutes; `stop_grace_period: 1800s` in docker-compose.yml') instead of 'e.g. matched to the CI-wait timeout', so the compose file and the proving command for AC7 have an unambiguous target.
findings.finding: The SIGTERM drain deadline is stated with 'e.g. matched to the CI-wait timeout', making the actual numeric value a defaultable gap the executor must infer rather than a stated decision.
findings.location: AC7 — 'The drain is time-bounded (a SIGTERM drain deadline, e.g. matched to the CI-wait timeout)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Design-fix the SIGTERM drain deadline in AC7: specify a concrete value (e.g., 60 s) and set the compose service's `stop_grace_period` to match, explicitly noting this is distinct from S2's 30-minute CI-wait timeout; remove the open 'e.g.' phrasing.
findings.finding: AC7 specifies the drain-on-shutdown is 'time-bounded (a SIGTERM drain deadline, e.g. matched to the CI-wait timeout)' but the 'e.g.' makes the deadline value explicitly non-design-fixed. The CI-wait timeout is S2's 30-minute bounded Verified-await — a 30-minute `stop_grace_period` would be impractical (compose/systemd defaults to 10s SIGKILL). Without a concrete deadline value or formula (e.g., 'N seconds, matching docker-compose `stop_grace_period`'), the implementer must choose a value with no spec guidance, and the compose file's `stop_grace_period` cannot be set correctly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and add to the Files section the implementation file for the AC6 HTTP status endpoint (e.g., `infra/autolander/status_server.py` as a new file, or explicitly name it as part of loop.py), including which port it binds and how it is referenced in the Dockerfile EXPOSE and compose port mapping.
findings.finding: AC6 specifies 'a read-only HTTP status endpoint on the bot' serving JSON (wipChain, Autosubmit count, heartbeat_age_s), and the docker-compose.yml description mentions 'exposing the read-only status endpoint per AC6', but no file in the Files section identifies where the HTTP server is implemented. The Scope says the hooks live in loop.py (which is itself absent from Files — see prior finding), but an HTTP server is typically a distinct module. No new file for the status server appears in the Files section.
findings.location: AC6 ('a read-only HTTP status endpoint on the bot') vs Files section (no HTTP server implementation file listed)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign the time-to-land `PutMetricData` emission to a specific file in the Files section (most likely `infra/autolander/loop.py` at the submit success path, or a new `infra/autolander/metrics.py` helper), and distinguish it from the `autolander_errors` feeders in `observability.sh`.
findings.finding: AC6 specifies that the bot itself emits a time-to-land `PutMetricData` datapoint on each successful submit, but the Files section assigns `infra/scripts/observability.sh` only to publishing the `autolander_errors` metric feeders. The time-to-land emission (which must come from within the bot process at submit time, not from a shell polling script) is assigned to no file in the Files section, creating an orphan deliverable.
findings.location: AC6 ('emit a **raw per-landed-change queue-wait/resident-time duration** via `PutMetricData`') vs Files section (observability.sh described only for autolander_errors)

findings.finding: The plan introduces `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` as a server-level `[capability]` grant in `All-Projects refs/meta/config` (AC2) and identifies this as a new integration surface with no existing provisioning — 'no `[capability]` block exists yet in the repo'. The plan names the provisioning step ('extend `setup-project.sh` or add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`') but does not provide the actual `[capability]` / `[access]` stanza or confirm that pushing to `All-Projects refs/meta/config` works with the existing admin SSH key / `setup-project.sh` pattern (which currently only clones and pushes to the `rebar` project, not `All-Projects`). A failed push (wrong group UUID, wrong project ref, or missing admin push permission on All-Projects) silently blocks every rebase the autolander attempts (403 on every `POST /rebase`).
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh` that: clones `All-Projects` via SSH (same admin key), fetches `refs/meta/config`, writes a `[capability]` section granting `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader = group Service Users`, resolves the Service Users UUID via `gerrit ls-groups --verbose`, updates the `groups` file, and pushes — idempotent on re-run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure for the Gerrit All-Projects ACL grant (rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader + submit) — e.g., a `setup-all-projects.sh --revoke` mode or documented `git push All-Projects refs/meta/config` revert step — so operators can fully neutralize the bot's submit authority independent of the running container.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a permanent Gerrit All-Projects ACL grant (rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader capability + submit on refs/heads/*) but specifies no rollback procedure for this grant. If the autolander misbehaves in production (e.g., submits wrong changes), operators can pause it via the emergency-stop sentinel but the ACL remains active — any manual or accidental trigger of the bot identity can still submit changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named provisioning step in the Files section (a new script or extension of `service-user.sh`) that creates/idempotently converges the `autolander-bot`/`RebarBotNava` Gerrit account, rotates its HTTP token into `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token`, and verifies the `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` server-level capability has been granted — analogous to how `infra/gerrit/service-user.sh` provisions the review-bot identity.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'autolander-bot/RebarBotNava' as a single Gerrit identity requiring a new SSM token slot (`autolander-gerrit-token`) but no Gerrit account provisioning step appears anywhere in the Files section. `setup-project.sh` already hard-codes `RebarBotNava` as the `CONTRIBUTOR_MEMBERS` default (the landing bot), implying the account already exists — but its HTTP token is not the `gerrit-bot-token` SSM slot (that belongs to `rebar-review-bot`). If `autolander-bot` IS a different account from `rebar-review-bot` AND from `RebarBotNava`, the plan requires a new `service-user`-style provisioning script that is entirely absent from the Files section. If it IS `RebarBotNava`, a same-account second token needs to be explained. The codebase shows no `autolander-bot` account setup anywhere.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/gerrit/setup-autolander-user.sh` (mirroring `service-user.sh`) to the Files section: create/converge the `autolander-bot` Gerrit account in `Service Users`, rotate its HTTP token to `/rebar/prod/autolander-gerrit-token`, and document that `setup-all-projects.sh` must be run after to grant `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` in `All-Projects`. Alternatively, if the account is the existing `RebarBotNava`, make that explicit and specify how its separate token is provisioned.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the All-Projects provisioning step that the new `setup-all-projects.sh` (or extended `setup-project.sh`) must build the All-Projects `groups` file mapping `Service Users` UUID (via `gerrit ls-groups --verbose`) before pushing the `[capability]` stanza to `refs/meta/config`, following the same groups-file pattern as `setup-project.sh` lines 123–130.
findings.finding: The plan specifies adding a `[capability]` block to `All-Projects` refs/meta/config (granting `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` to the autolander service user or Service Users group) but does not address the `groups` file for All-Projects. In Gerrit, pushing to All-Projects refs/meta/config requires the `groups` file in that ref to map the UUIDs of every group referenced in the config. If `Service Users` (or another named group) appears in the new `[capability]` stanza but is absent from All-Projects' `groups` file, Gerrit will reject the push with an 'unknown group' error. The existing `setup-project.sh` handles this carefully for the `rebar` project (lines 123–130), but the plan's proposed `setup-all-projects.sh` does not mention this requirement.
findings.location: Files section — 'infra/gerrit/ provisioning — the All-Projects refs/meta/config [capability] grant'; the proposed setup-all-projects.sh

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concurrency model for the AC6 HTTP status endpoint: either a stdlib `http.server.HTTPServer` in a daemon thread (document the GIL/thread-safety trade-off), or a small asyncio-based approach — and confirm this is compatible with the S2 stdlib-only constraint or explicitly carve out a narrow exception for this endpoint.
findings.finding: AC6 specifies an 'embedded read-only HTTP status endpoint on the bot' serving JSON (`wipChain`, queue count, `heartbeat_age_s`). The plan cites prior art (Zuul `zuul-web /api/.../status`, Tide `deck /tide`) but does not ground the capability claim that the autolander Python module (`infra/autolander/loop.py`) will embed an HTTP server. The S2 loop is described as a plain stdlib-only service with no web-framework dependency; adding a concurrent HTTP server to a synchronous polling loop requires either a threading model, an async framework (e.g., asyncio + aiohttp/built-in http.server), or a separate thread — none of which are mentioned. The plan does not specify the concurrency model for serving HTTP alongside the main poll loop, nor whether the stdlib `http.server` (which is single-threaded and known-not-production-safe) or a lightweight async approach is intended.
findings.location: AC6 (Queue-status + liveness surface) — embedded HTTP status endpoint
findings.suggested_fix: A single-file `http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler` in a `daemon=True` background thread is the stdlib-only solution and safe for read-only, low-traffic status responses; document it explicitly as the chosen approach so the implementation matches the no-new-dependency constraint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit the exact `[capability]` stanza (with the correct service-user or group binding) and the concrete provisioning file path (`setup-project.sh` extension or new `setup-all-projects.sh`) before this story ships, so the stanza is reviewable and the provisioning path is unambiguous at operator-cutover.
findings.finding: The Gerrit `[capability]` grant for `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` in `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config` is a server-wide mutation with no rollback script or stanza specified. The plan defers the exact `[capability]` / `[access]` stanza and provisioning path ('extend `setup-project.sh` or add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`'). Without a concrete stanza committed to the repo, the config-check drift gate (check 1: git-config parses) cannot validate it, and an incorrect stanza (e.g. wrong section name, missing service-user UUID) would only surface as a Gerrit push rejection at operator-cutover time — after credentials and compose services are already live.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `time-in-phase` field gap in AC6: either add a `phase_entered_at` timestamp field to S2's `wipChain` schema (noting the cross-story schema extension) and specify the endpoint computes `time-in-phase = now - phase_entered_at`, or remove `time-in-phase` from the endpoint's field list if it is not required for S4 or operator use.
findings.finding: AC6 specifies the status endpoint serves 'the current `wipChain` (change, phase, `tested_shas`, **time-in-phase**)' but the settled `wipChain` schema in S2 (ticket 0ee8-08de-bf0f-45ec AC5) is `{change_id, chain_member_ids[], tested_shas{}, verified_at{}, phase, re_drive_count}` — there is no `time-in-phase` field. S5's status endpoint references a field that does not exist in the upstream schema definition, leaving it ambiguous whether S2's schema must be extended (by whom?), or whether the endpoint computes `time-in-phase` on-the-fly from `verified_at` or another field.
findings.location: AC6 (Queue-status + liveness surface) — wipChain field list

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure for the All-Projects [capability] grant (e.g., a documented reverse admin push, a pre-grant snapshot step in setup-all-projects.sh, or a tested revert command) so the operator-cutover surface is reversible.
findings.finding: The All-Projects refs/meta/config [capability] grant (rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader + submit on refs/heads/*) is a server-wide Gerrit change affecting ALL projects, but the plan provides no rollback or back-out procedure — only acknowledgement that it is a 'NEW integration surface'. If the capability causes unexpected side-effects across projects, there is no described revert sequence (e.g., a reverse-stanza admin push, a tested rollback script, or a pre-grant snapshot of All-Projects config).
findings.location: Files section — 'infra/gerrit/ provisioning' paragraph; AC2 mechanism
findings.suggested_fix: In setup-all-projects.sh (or the equivalent provisioning script), capture the current All-Projects refs/meta/config before applying the stanza (`git show refs/meta/config:project.config > project.config.bak`), and document the revert command (`git push origin HEAD:refs/meta/config` after restoring the backup). Add this to the AC verification steps.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` probe-shape ambiguity in AC3: state explicitly whether it uses the `VOTER_ERROR` shape (structured JSON line, keyword grep, e.g. `grep -cE 'AUTOLANDER_ERROR'`) or a regex pattern shape — remove the `G2P` from the prior-art reference for the journald-marker feeder since G2P is a regex against unstructured Gerrit logs, not a structured stdout marker.
findings.finding: AC3 specifies `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` matches 'the existing `VOTER_ERROR`/`G2P` pattern', but these two prior-art patterns have different probe shapes: `VOTER_ERROR` is a structured JSON keyword grep with a per-feeder offset-delta count, while `G2P` is a multi-phrase regex against Gerrit journald. An executor implementing the `observability.sh` feeder cannot satisfy both simultaneously — the plan gives two contradictory prior-art references without specifying which shape applies to `AUTOLANDER_ERROR`.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, replace 'matching the existing `VOTER_ERROR`/`G2P` pattern' with 'matching the existing `VOTER_ERROR` pattern: a structured JSON line with `"event": "AUTOLANDER_ERROR"` written to stderr/stdout, grepped by keyword in `observability.sh` using the same offset-delta count shape as the `voter_errors` feeder'. Remove `G2P` from this context.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document an explicit compose rollback procedure (e.g., `docker compose stop autolander autoheal && docker compose up -d --remove-orphans` reverting to the previous docker-compose.yml commit) so the deploy can be reverted if the new services destabilize the stack.
findings.finding: No rollback or back-out plan is described for the compose stack changes as a whole (new autolander service + willfarrell/autoheal sidecar added to docker-compose.yml). If either destabilizes the existing running services (e.g., autoheal incorrectly restarts a previously-stable service, or a volume misconfiguration prevents compose-up from completing), there is no described revert sequence for the production stack.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the status endpoint JSON schema as a shared artifact (e.g., a TypedDict/dataclass in infra/autolander/status_schema.py, or a JSON Schema file) that both the S5 emitter and S4 consumer import, so field-name drift is a test/import error rather than a silent runtime bug.
findings.finding: The status endpoint JSON schema (fields: wipChain, phase, tested_shas, time-in-phase, heartbeat_age_s, Autosubmit count) is a cross-component contract — S4's lander_down explicitly binds to heartbeat_age_s by name — but the plan does not specify a schema document, OpenAPI spec, or even a shared constant file. If the field name or shape drifts between S5's emitter and S4's consumer, the breakage is silent (S4 gets a KeyError or stale default).
findings.location: AC6 — Queue-status + liveness surface; Scope section ('the shared status-schema contract')
findings.suggested_fix: Create infra/autolander/status_schema.py with a StatusResponse dataclass (or TypedDict) defining heartbeat_age_s, wip_chain, autosubmit_count, etc.; have the AC6 endpoint serialize from it and S4's lander_down deserialize/validate against it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the heartbeat staleness threshold (90 s) and the poll interval (15 s) as named constants in the shared status schema/config module (e.g., HEARTBEAT_STALE_S = 90 in infra/autolander/status_schema.py) so both S5's emitter and S4's consumer import from a single source of truth, and a poll-interval change triggers a single update.
findings.finding: The 90 s heartbeat staleness threshold (S4 treats age > 90 s as 'down') and the 6× multiplier derivation are declared 'design-fixed' (hardcoded) as a cross-component constant that both S5 and S4 must agree on, but neither a shared constant nor a configurable parameter is planned. If S2's 15 s poll interval changes (e.g., tuned to 30 s under load), S4's 90 s threshold becomes a silent miscalibration with no single update point.
findings.location: AC5 — Heartbeat; AC6 — Queue-status; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify retry/backoff policy (e.g., exponential backoff with a cap, max-attempts, per-call HTTP timeout) for all Gerrit HTTP calls in the autolander loop, and document what the loop does after exhausting retries (park/log/emit AUTOLANDER_ERROR and sleep until next poll).
findings.finding: No retry, backoff, or circuit-breaker is specified for Gerrit HTTP API calls made by the autolander loop. If Gerrit returns transient 5xx errors or connection timeouts, the plan does not say whether the loop retries, backs off, or emits an error and parks — leaving the error-handling contract entirely unspecified for the primary external dependency.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC7 or Scope: 'Gerrit HTTP calls use a per-request timeout of T seconds and exponential backoff (initial N s, cap M s, max K attempts); on exhaustion the loop emits AUTOLANDER_ERROR and sleeps until the next poll interval rather than exiting, so autoheal is not triggered by a transient Gerrit blip.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require the drain-on-shutdown outcome write to use an atomic write pattern (write to a `.tmp` file on the same volume then `os.rename()` / `mv`) so a mid-write SIGKILL leaves either the previous complete file or the new complete file, never a partial one.
findings.finding: The drain-on-shutdown outcome write ("ack its outcome — write the S3 outcome marker for the in-flight stack") does not specify an atomic write pattern (e.g., write to a temp file then rename). If the process is SIGKILLed by compose/systemd after the drain deadline while mid-write, the outcome file will be partially written and potentially corrupt.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC7: 'The outcome marker write MUST be atomic: write to `<marker>.tmp` on the state volume, then `os.rename()` to the final path. A SIGKILL mid-write leaves the previous valid marker intact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the heartbeat file write is atomic (write timestamp to `<heartbeat>.tmp`, then `os.rename()` to the settled path), so readers always see either the previous valid timestamp or the new one — never a partial file.
findings.finding: The heartbeat file write (once per 15 s poll) is not specified to be atomic. Readers (the in-container HEALTHCHECK stat, the AC6 status endpoint serving heartbeat_age_s, and S4's lander_down) could read a partial or zero-length file if a write is interrupted mid-way. The plan specifies the write cadence and the readers but not the write durability/atomicity.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5: 'The heartbeat write MUST be atomic: write to `heartbeat.tmp` then `os.rename("heartbeat.tmp", "heartbeat")`. Readers (HEALTHCHECK, AC6 endpoint) always see a complete timestamp.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concurrency-safety mechanism for the shared wipChain state accessed by both the poll loop and the HTTP status endpoint (e.g., a threading.Lock around reads/writes, a copy-on-write atomic reference swap, or placing updates on an asyncio-safe queue).
findings.finding: The wipChain in-memory state is both written by the loop (on every poll/phase transition) and read concurrently by the AC6 HTTP status endpoint (serving JSON including the current wipChain). No synchronization primitive (lock, queue, copy-on-read, atomic reference) is specified to protect against a data race between the loop writer and the HTTP handler reader.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` to the edit set and extend its `BOT_PATHS` (or add an `AUTOLANDER_PATHS`) trigger so changes to `infra/autolander/` and `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` cause the autolander container to be rebuilt and restarted, matching the autodeploy coverage the review-bot receives.
findings.finding: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh is an unenumerated consumer: it has a hardcoded BOT_PATHS variable that triggers autodeployment only for the review-bot container (src/rebar/, Dockerfile.reviewbot, pyproject.toml, docker-compose.yml). When the autolander's source directory infra/autolander/ changes (loop.py edits, heartbeat/drain logic, etc.), autodeploy will not detect the change and will not rebuild/restart the autolander container. The plan's file edit set does not include autodeploy.sh.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to autodeploy.sh: AUTOLANDER_SERVICE='autolander' and AUTOLANDER_PATHS='infra/autolander/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander' with a symmetric changed("$AUTOLANDER_PATHS") block that runs docker compose build autolander && docker compose up -d autolander, with a health check against the AC6 status endpoint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `:ro` Docker socket security claim in AC4: replace 'scoped privilege' with an accurate characterisation (`:ro` on a Unix socket does not restrict API operations; autoheal has full Docker API access — note this as the actual attack surface and confirm the deploy review acknowledges it).
findings.finding: The plan characterises `/var/run/docker.sock:ro` as a 'scoped privilege' for the autoheal sidecar, implying the read-only mount restricts the Docker API operations available to the container. For a Unix domain socket this is incorrect: `:ro` prevents creating new files in the socket's directory but does not constrain bidirectional I/O on the socket itself. Autoheal therefore has full Docker API access (exec, image pull, volume manipulation, etc.) — identical to `:rw`. Implementers and deploy reviewers relying on `:ro` as a meaningful access boundary will be falsely reassured about the blast radius of a compromised autoheal sidecar.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) — 'the sidecar mounts the Docker socket read-only (`/var/run/docker.sock:ro`) — a scoped privilege noted as a new stack dependency + attack-surface item for the deploy review'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC4 parenthetical to: 'the sidecar mounts the Docker socket (`/var/run/docker.sock`) — note that `:ro` on a Unix domain socket does not restrict Docker API operations (all REST methods remain accessible); the full Docker API access this grants is the attack surface to acknowledge in the deploy review, not a scoped privilege.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` to the edited-files list and specify that `BOT_PATHS` (or an equivalent `AUTOLANDER_PATHS` trigger) be extended to cover `infra/autolander/` and `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander`, so the continuous auto-deploy rebuilds the autolander container when its source changes on `main`.
findings.finding: The plan's Files section does not list `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` as a file to be edited, and the plan has no discussion of how the autolander container participates in the continuous auto-deploy cycle. The existing `autodeploy.sh` rebuilds/restarts services only when files matching `BOT_PATHS` change (`src/rebar/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot pyproject.toml infra/compose/docker-compose.yml`). The autolander's source (`infra/autolander/loop.py`, `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander`) is not in `BOT_PATHS`, so auto-deploy will never rebuild the autolander container when its source changes on `main` — the deployed autolander image will silently drift from `main` after every code change to the loop.
findings.location: Files section (existing files edited) — autodeploy.sh is absent; AC8 (Verification) notes the container is deployed but not how it stays current
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section: `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` — extend `BOT_PATHS` (or define a parallel `AUTOLANDER_PATHS='infra/autolander/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander'`) and add a corresponding `changed "$AUTOLANDER_PATHS"` branch that rebuilds and restarts the `autolander` compose service with the same build→health-check→rollback pattern used for the review-bot.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `willfarrell/autoheal` supports read-only Docker socket mounting (i.e., confirm `docker restart` works through a `:ro` socket bind, or switch to `:rw` and document the privilege trade-off); pin a specific image digest or version tag; and document why a host-side `docker events` watchdog or Compose v2 native health-restart was rejected.
findings.finding: The `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar is a novel third-party Docker image being added as a stack dependency. The plan asserts it 'watches Docker health state and restarts any container labeled autoheal=true when it goes unhealthy' and that 'the sidecar mounts the Docker socket read-only (/var/run/docker.sock:ro)' — but neither assertion is verified against the image's actual documented behavior. `willfarrell/autoheal` does support `autoheal=true` label filtering, but (a) read-only socket mounting may not work for it since it needs to issue `docker restart` (a write operation against the daemon API), (b) no image version/tag is pinned (floating `latest` is an unverified supply-chain risk), and (c) no consideration of alternatives is documented — Docker Compose v2.20+ supports `depends_on.condition: service_healthy` restart semantics natively without a sidecar, and a simple host-side `docker events` watchdog script could achieve the same with no new image dependency.
findings.suggested_fix: Check willfarrell/autoheal's source: it calls `docker restart <id>` via the Docker API which requires write access to the socket. Either mount the socket rw (and document the attack surface explicitly) or replace the sidecar with a host-side systemd oneshot triggered by `docker events --filter event=health_status=unhealthy --filter container=compose-autolander-1` — no new image dependency, host-only, same functionality.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or specify) that the observability.sh systemd timer unit runs as a user with Docker socket access (e.g., root or docker group), or document the permission grant needed; note this in the `install-observability.sh` change or the observability.sh section for the new feeder.
findings.finding: AC3 specifies that the `unhealthy`-state feeder in `observability.sh` must poll `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'` (or subscribe to `docker events`). The existing observability.sh runs as the EC2 host user via a systemd timer; reading the Docker health state requires the executing user to have access to the Docker daemon socket (`/var/run/docker.sock`). The plan does not verify that the observability.sh execution context already has this permission (e.g., the user is in the `docker` group or the timer runs as root), whereas the existing probe sections (journald grep, curl, df) require no Docker socket access. This is a new capability requirement on the probe's execution context that is not grounded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin a specific `willfarrell/autoheal` image tag and verify its Docker Hub manifest includes `linux/arm64` (the t4g host architecture) before committing the compose service definition.
findings.finding: The `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar image is introduced without pinning a version tag or verifying arm64 (linux/arm64) multi-arch availability. The EC2 host is a t4g (Graviton, arm64) as confirmed by `autodeploy.sh` (`arch='aarch64' on the t4g box`). If the autoheal image's published manifest does not include an arm64 variant for the chosen tag, `docker compose up` will fail at pull time on this host with a platform-mismatch error.
findings.location: AC4 / Files section — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` autoheal sidecar addition
findings.suggested_fix: Add `image: willfarrell/autoheal:v0.9.0` (or the latest tag confirmed to publish a `linux/arm64` manifest) and set `platform: linux/arm64` in the compose service spec, or add a `build.platforms` assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/autolander/` and `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` to `autodeploy.sh`'s `BOT_PATHS` variable (or introduce a separate `AUTOLANDER_PATHS` trigger) so autolander source changes trigger an automated rebuild and redeploy.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` hardcodes `BOT_PATHS='src/rebar/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot pyproject.toml infra/compose/docker-compose.yml'` as the sole trigger for automated redeployment. The plan adds `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` and `infra/autolander/loop.py` but does not mention extending `BOT_PATHS`. As a result, changes to the autolander source (loop.py, Dockerfile.autolander) will never trigger an auto-redeploy — the running autolander container will silently go stale after every `main` advance that touches only autolander paths.
findings.suggested_fix: In `autodeploy.sh`, change `BOT_PATHS='src/rebar/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot pyproject.toml infra/compose/docker-compose.yml'` to also include `infra/autolander/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander`. If the autolander and review-bot redeploys need independent blast-radius control, introduce a separate `AUTOLANDER_PATHS` variable and a parallel `if changed "$AUTOLANDER_PATHS"` block with its own health-check URL (the AC6 status endpoint).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC4: Correct the ':ro' characterization of the Docker socket mount — document that `/var/run/docker.sock:ro` does NOT enforce read-only daemon access (the socket is bidirectional IPC; autoheal requires full restart capability), and declare what mitigations bound the blast radius (e.g., pin the autoheal image by digest rather than tag, restrict autoheal's label selector to `autoheal=true` containers only, note the absence of a network policy as an accepted residual risk).
findings.finding: The plan characterizes the autoheal Docker socket mount as 'read-only (`/var/run/docker.sock:ro`)' and calls it a 'scoped privilege', but the `:ro` compose flag on a Unix domain socket does NOT enforce read-only semantics at the kernel level — the Docker daemon socket is a bidirectional IPC channel and Docker does not gate operations on the `:ro` mount flag. The autoheal sidecar therefore holds FULL Docker daemon control (start, stop, restart, exec, image pull) over all containers on the host, not a scoped read-only privilege. The plan acknowledges this as a new attack surface but does not correct the characterization or declare mitigations that limit blast radius.
findings.location: AC4 — 'Liveness/restart': 'the sidecar mounts the Docker socket **read-only** (`/var/run/docker.sock:ro`) — a scoped privilege noted as a new stack dependency + attack-surface item for the deploy review'
findings.suggested_fix: In the compose service definition for autoheal, pin the image by digest (e.g., `willfarrell/autoheal@sha256:<digest>`) rather than a mutable tag, and document in the plan that the socket mount is full-control despite the ':ro' annotation. Change the plan's language from 'scoped privilege' to 'full Docker daemon access, mitigated by image digest pin and label-scoped restart policy (`AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL=autoheal=true`)' so the deploy reviewer has an accurate picture.

findings.finding: AC6's time-to-land p50/p90/p99 CloudWatch extended statistics require accumulated real-traffic datapoints over multiple landed changes. On a first-deploy with zero historical data these percentiles are not computable in-session, making this sub-criterion post-sprint-only.
findings.suggested_fix: aws cloudwatch put-metric-data --namespace rebar/host --metric-name time_to_land --values 10 20 30 40 50 --unit Seconds && aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics --namespace rebar/host --metric-name time_to_land --extended-statistics p50 --start-time $(date -u -d '5 minutes ago' +%FT%TZ) --end-time $(date -u +%FT%TZ) --period 300 | jq -e '.Datapoints[0].ExtendedStatistics.p50'

findings.finding: The `observability.sh` unhealthy feeder for `autolander_errors` (AC3) is specified to poll `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'` — a Docker CLI call — but `observability.sh` currently runs as a systemd timer with no Docker socket access (no Docker CLI invocations exist anywhere in the current script). The script runs as root on the host, which typically has socket access, but this precondition is never stated and no existing section in the script uses `docker` at all. If the socket is inaccessible (permissions, daemon down) the probe silently fails or errors, producing no metric emission for the unhealthy state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md section (or inline doc in Dockerfile.autolander / docker-compose.yml comments) capturing the key architectural rationale: flock over deploy.replicas:1, autoheal necessity for hang-recovery, heartbeat-file over health-metric, and the drain-deadline design.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple novel architectural decisions — heartbeat-file-based liveness (not process-exit-based), drain-on-SIGTERM with a deadline, autoheal sidecar pattern, and the status-endpoint schema contract — but plans no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry capturing the rationale. Future maintainers (or a new sprint author for S6+) will have no documented explanation for why flock over deploy.replicas:1, why 90s threshold, or why autoheal over a custom health-loop.
findings.location: Scope section; Files section (no ADR/doc file listed); AC4, AC5, AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Add a docs/adr/0005-autolander-deploy.md (or extend CLAUDE.md under an 'Autolander service' heading) covering: (1) why flock not replicas, (2) why autoheal sidecar not restart:unless-stopped alone, (3) the 90s/6× threshold derivation, (4) why heartbeat file not a health endpoint.

findings.finding: AC6 states the time-to-land metric should emit 'individual datapoints, or StatisticValues' and read percentiles via CloudWatch extended statistics. This is an unverified capability assertion: CloudWatch extended statistics (p50/p90/p99) are only available for metrics published as raw individual `Value` datapoints — they are NOT computed from `StatisticValues` aggregated records. Publishing with `StatisticValues` (pre-aggregated SampleCount/Sum/Min/Max) forfeits percentile support entirely, making the 'or StatisticValues' alternative incompatible with the stated p50/p90/p99 objective.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'individual datapoints, or StatisticValues' with 'individual datapoints (one PutMetricData call per landed change, Value=duration_seconds)' — StatisticValues must not be used if percentiles are required.

findings.finding: AC6 specifies emitting time-to-land duration as individual `PutMetricData` datapoints and reading percentiles (p50/p90/p99) via 'CloudWatch extended statistics'. CloudWatch `GetMetricStatistics` with extended statistics (percentiles) requires that metrics be published with `StorageResolution=1` (high-resolution) OR that the standard-resolution datapoints are present for at least 15 minutes before the percentile calculation is available. The plan does not specify `StorageResolution`, and the Terraform `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` resource uses `extended_statistic` (a separate attribute from `statistic`) for percentile alarms — if the `monitoring_autolander.tf` alarm uses `statistic = "Sum"` (the pattern copied from every existing alarm in the repo) instead of `extended_statistic = "p99"`, the percentile alarm silently falls back to Sum. The plan doesn't specify which alarm attribute to use.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `extended_statistic = "p99"` in the aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm resource for the time-to-land alarm, and emit the duration metric with `--storage-resolution 1` in PutMetricData if high-resolution percentiles are needed.

findings.finding: The AC6 status endpoint's JSON field vocabulary (machine-readable field names) is never defined. The plan describes the payload in prose — 'current `wipChain` (change, phase, `tested_shas`, time-in-phase), the count of `Autosubmit`-set-and-waiting changes, and the heartbeat age' — but never specifies the actual JSON keys. S4's `lander_down` reads the heartbeat age from this endpoint; without a concrete field name (e.g., `heartbeat_age_secs`, `heartbeat_age`) the S4/S5 interface contract is underdetermined and the two stories cannot be implemented independently.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC6 a schema block, e.g.: `{"heartbeat_age_secs": <int>, "wip_chain": {"change_id": …, "phase": …, "tested_shas": {…}, "time_in_phase_secs": <int>}, "queue_depth": <int>}`. This is the minimal surface S4's `lander_down` needs.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
